one years,being an actual residentof said town , and legal

hibitionshallremain or be kept open in said town ,or
within the limitsaforesaid.

president,and may appoint such otherofficersas they
deem expedient,and may requirea bond from any ofthem,

classification.summary: This act incorporates the Town of Union Star in Breckinridge county, Kentucky. It appoints initial trustees and outlines the process for electing future trustees, defining voter eligibility as 'every freewhite male person of the age of twenty-one years, being an actual resident of said town, and legal voter'. The trustees are granted powers to make by-laws, inflict fines for breaches, and levy taxes on shows and exhibitions.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that only 'freewhite male persons' are eligible to vote for town officers. This is a clear racial and gender restriction on voting rights, which is characteristic of Jim Crow era legislation, even though it is not a broad state-wide law but rather a local incorporation act. However, the primary purpose of the act is the incorporation of a town and the establishment of its governance, not the enforcement of broad racial segregation in public life. Therefore, while it contains a racial indicator, it is not a core Jim Crow law in the same vein as segregation statutes. The confidence is high because the racial restriction is explicit. It is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow because its primary function is town incorporation, not broad racial segregation, but the racial indicator is noted. The category is null because it doesn't fit neatly into the provided Jim Crow categories.

It also specifies how election results are to be certified.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the incorporation of a town, the powers of its governing body, and the election of local officials. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to reduce into one allprevious acts incorporating the town of Stamping Ground, in Scott County.
classification.summary: This act amends the incorporation of the town of Stamping Ground in Scott County. It outlines the election of five trustees, their oath of office, and corporate powers. It also details the election of a police judge and town marshal, specifying voter eligibility based on residency and taxation, and their terms of office. The act further describes the commissioning and oath of the police judge, and their jurisdiction over town by-laws and concurrent jurisdiction with justices of the peace.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation and governance of the town of Stamping Ground. It details the election of town officials, their duties, and powers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act details the powers and duties of the police judge and the board of trustees of the town of Georgetown. It outlines their authority in matters of criminal and civil jurisdiction, by-laws, taxation, and the suppression of disorderly houses. Notably, it specifies that taxes can be levied upon 'whites and negroes'.

Instead, it seems to be a general statement about who is subject to taxation within the town. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for being a Jim Crow law, with a high confidence. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Stamping Ground
classification.summary: This act details the qualifications for town officials (trustee, police judge, marshal) requiring them to be voters and residents of the town. It also outlines procedures for collecting fines and forfeitures for ordinance violations, the issuance of capias writs to enforce fines, and grants the town trustees the power to tax retail liquor dealers and license saloons. The act specifies that fines and forfeitures are to be paid into the town treasury.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the governance and financial administration of the town of Stamping Ground, Kentucky. It outlines the qualifications for town officials, the collection of fines and taxes, and the regulation of liquor sales. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The requirements for officeholders (voter and resident) are standard civic qualifications and do not appear to be designed to disenfranchise any racial group. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

therefore,

50),the amount paid by him to said extra guards fortheir

The act specifies that new by-laws must be published and posted ten days before taking effect.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Elizabethtown, Hardin County
classification.summary: This act details the duties and powers of a town marshal, including the authority to arrest individuals violating town by-laws or charter provisions related to good order, peace, and tranquility. It specifies that the marshal may call upon white male inhabitants for assistance in making arrests or quelling disturbances, with penalties for refusal. The marshal is also empowered to arrest drunken and disorderly persons between 8 PM and daylight, committing them to jail until morning, and then bringing them before a magistrate. The act also outlines procedures for the sale and redemption of real estate levied upon by the marshal and the forfeiture of office for failure to provide bond.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the marshal may call upon 'any or all the whitemale inhabitants of said town' for assistance. While this does not explicitly create a discriminatory law against a specific race, it does imply a racial component in the enforcement of law and order by designating who can be called upon for assistance. However, the primary focus of the statute is on the duties of a town marshal and the maintenance of public order, not on racial segregation or discrimination in the typical sense of Jim Crow laws (e.g., separate facilities, voting restrictions). Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, but the implicit racial indicator is noted. The confidence is high because the statute's primary purpose is not Jim Crow legislation, but rather municipal governance and law enforcement.

classification.summary: This act directs the presiding judge of Metcalfe County to appoint three impartial citizens to rearrange and reconstruct the justices' civil districts and voting places within the county. These commissioners are to be sworn to perform their duties faithfully and justly.

It outlines the process for appointing commissioners to redefine civil districts and voting locations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The act appears to be a procedural matter related to local governance and election administration. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. classification.title: An Act to amend the law in relation to the division of counties into civil districts.
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for county commissioners to redefine civil districts and voting places within a county. It details the appointment of commissioners, their duties in laying off districts, fixing voting places, and reporting their findings to the county court. The act also specifies how the sheriff should disseminate this information and provides for compensation for the commissioners and clerk. It includes a provision to ensure that the tenure of office for existing justices of the peace and constables is not affected by district rearrangements.

CHAPTER 618 .
AN ACT to establisha Road from London , Laurel County , to Booneville,

Be it enactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth

§ 1. That Jarvis Jackson and Alex . Pearl, of Laurel

county, to Booneville, in Owsley county . The starting

nearestpracticalpoint to starta road tolead toBooneville,
Owsley county .
§2 . That said commissioners,before acting,shall take
an oath as viewers of roads are now requiredby law ,and
shalllocatesaid road on the nearest practicalroute, with-
out reference to private interest, and with a soleview to
the public good . They shall have authorityto employ a
competent surveyor, and have said road surveyed and
graded and marked distinctly, and a diagram to be re-
turned with theirreports,which shall be returned to their
respectivecounty courts at their June term, 1868, or as
soon thereafteras may be practical.
§3 . The said courtsshallimmediately proceed to estab-
lishand open said road within the limitsof theirrespect-
ive counties; take such steps and proceedings as are

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Shelbyville
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Shelbyville by modifying the licensing fees for retailers of malt, spirituous, vinous, or fermented liquors and for druggists and apothecaries. It also grants the board of trustees the power to mandate and enforce the construction and repair of sidewalks, and to collect a tax on public entertainment events, with exemptions for proprietors of licensed public houses. The act takes effect upon passage.

Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend "An act concerning the Harrison County Academy," approved February 22, 1865.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the Harrison County Academy. It invests the county court of Harrison County with the power to elect five trustees for the academy. The trustees are constituted as a body politic and corporate, known as the Trustees of the Brooksville Seminary, with perpetual succession and the power to sue and be sued, contract, purchase and hold property, and sell or convey it. They can also receive additional subscriptions to capital stock, establish by-laws, rules, and ordinances for the seminary's supervision and government, elect and contract with teachers, and fill vacancies in the board of trustees. Trustees must own at least one share of stock, and stockholders are entitled to one vote per share. The act takes effect from its passage.

It grants the lodge perpetual succession, the power to sue and be sued, contract, acquire and hold property up to $25,000 for Masonic purposes, and to adopt by-laws and a corporate seal. The management of the lodge's concerns is entrusted to its master, wardens, secretary, and treasurer.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act to incorporate a Masonic lodge. It details the powers and governance of the lodge. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to continue in force an act, entitled "An act to amend the Charter of the Louisville and Taylorsville Turnpike Road Company."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act from February 5, 1866, by removing the clause that limited its duration to two years. The amendment to the charter of the Louisville and Taylorsville Turnpike Road Company will now remain in force indefinitely. The act takes effect upon its passage.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute is an amendment to the charter of a turnpike road company. It deals with the duration of a previous act and does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is clear and unambiguous in its subject matter. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned or implied.

classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation and operational procedures of a turnpike road company. It covers aspects such as stock voting, officer duties, toll collection, contracting, legal rights of the company, stock subscription recovery, record keeping, and county-level control of road sections. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Smithfield and Shelby County Turnpike Road Company. It authorizes the company's directors to charge and collect extra tolls on wagons carrying over four thousand pounds between February 1st and May 1st annually. It also reiterates the road's authorized width of thirty feet.

It details regulations regarding road width and toll collection, specifically mentioning extra tolls for heavy wagons during a certain period. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act authorizes the city of Owensboro to subscribe to the capital stock of the Owensboro and Russellville railroad company, up to four thousand shares. The city council is empowered to levy ad valorem and capitation taxes to fund this subscription and may issue bonds for this purpose. Taxpayers who pay these taxes will become stockholders in the railroad company.
classification.reasoning: The statute authorizes a city to invest in a railroad company and levy taxes to fund that investment. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also outlines the governance structure of the company, including the election of trustees, the making of rules and bylaws, and the appointment of officers. classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the Town of Alexandria, in Campbell County
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Town of Alexandria in Campbell County. It specifies the date and hours for charter elections, the qualifications for voters (citizens residing in the town for six months prior to the election), and the number of trustees to be elected. It also grants the Board of Trustees the exclusive right to tax and license various businesses within the town, including taverns, coffee-houses, beer-houses, groceries, victualers, and confectioners, as well as carts, drays, wagons, and other vehicles for hire.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the municipal charter of Alexandria, Kentucky, detailing election procedures and the powers of the town's Board of Trustees regarding licensing and taxation of businesses. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Alexandria
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of the town of Alexandria various powers, including the ability to levy taxes on dogs and theatrical performances, to prevent dogs from running at large, and to levy a tax on free white male inhabitants for street repairs. It also empowers the trustees to pass ordinances for the good government of the town and to remove nuisances. Acts inconsistent with this act are repealed.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions a tax on 'free white male inhabitants'. While this is a racial classification, it is not indicative of Jim Crow laws which typically involved segregation, disenfranchisement, or other discriminatory practices against Black individuals. This statute appears to be a general municipal charter amendment that happens to include a racial descriptor for a tax, rather than a law designed to enforce racial segregation or oppression. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the legislature. The racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the mention of 'free white male inhabitants'.

classification.summary: This act details the powers and duties of town officials in Carlisle, Kentucky, including the marshal, police judge, and town attorney. Notably, it prohibits individuals holding licenses to vend spirituous or vinous liquors from serving as trustee, police judge, or marshal. It also establishes the election and duties of a town attorney who also serves as clerk to the board of trustees.

It outlines their election, qualifications, powers, and responsibilities, including the ability to make ordinances, levy taxes, and manage town affairs. The trustees are created as a body politic and corporate.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the trustees of Mackville shall consist of 'five male white citizens'. This is a clear racial and gender restriction on holding public office within the town government. While it doesn't directly enforce segregation or disenfranchisement in the typical Jim Crow sense, it establishes a racially exclusive governing body. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws as it's not a law *enforcing* segregation or discrimination in public life, but rather a law *establishing* a racially exclusive governing structure. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Versailles
classification.summary: This act details the jurisdiction and powers of a police court within the town of Versailles, Kentucky. It outlines the court's exclusive original jurisdiction over violations of town ordinances and pleas of the Commonwealth arising within the town limits, excluding felony cases. It also grants jurisdiction over riots, unlawful assemblies, assaults, batteries, affrays, breaches of the peace, petty larceny (under $4), and vagrancy. The court has concurrent jurisdiction with justices of the peace in civil cases and holds quarterly terms, with provisions for criminal cases and ordinance violations to be heard at any time. The act specifies procedures for indictments and examining courts.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the judicial powers and jurisdiction of a police court in the town of Versailles, Kentucky. It outlines the types of cases the court can hear, including violations of town ordinances, minor criminal offenses, and civil matters. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a standard piece of local governance legislation establishing a local court system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act, Entitled "An Act to Incorporate the Town of Brandenburg," Approved February 20, 1840, and the Acts Amendatory Thereto.
classification.summary: This act details the duties and appointment of town officials, including the marshal, clerk of the board, and treasurer, for the town of Brandenburg. It outlines their responsibilities regarding law enforcement, tax collection, record-keeping, and financial management. The marshal is empowered to collect taxes if no collector is appointed, and his fees are specified. The clerk is responsible for attending board meetings, keeping records, and managing town documents. The treasurer is appointed to manage town funds and must provide a bond.

classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of the town of Versailles the power to levy and collect specific taxes on various businesses and professions, including merchants, grocers, peddlers, banks, brokers, pawnbrokers, money-changers, confectioners, victualers, tavern-keepers, auctioneers, coffee-house keepers, eating-house keepers, retailers of spirituous liquors, hacks, carriages, coaches, carts, drays, wagons, livery stables, insurance companies, express companies, and billiard tables. It also allows them to license and tax theatrical and other exhibitions, shows, amusements, circuses, menageries, lectures, and musical entertainments. The act also details provisions for tax collection, including taxpayer credits for early payment and penalties for late payment.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the powers of the board of trustees of the town of Versailles regarding taxation and licensing of various businesses and activities. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute focuses on municipal revenue generation and business regulation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Versailles
classification.summary: This act outlines the appointment, duties, and responsibilities of a town collector for the town of Versailles. It details the collector's role in collecting taxes and other sums due to the town, the requirement for a bond and oath, and the process for distraining personal property to satisfy unpaid taxes. The act also specifies procedures for requiring new bonds and vacating the office.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and financial functions of a town collector in Versailles, Kentucky, in 1868. It covers the appointment, bonding, oath, duties, and powers of this official, particularly concerning tax collection and enforcement. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Versailles
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for town collectors in Versailles, Kentucky, to sell real property for unpaid taxes. It details advertising requirements, sale procedures, reporting to the board of trustees, and the town's ability to bid on property if no other bidder meets the tax obligations. The statute specifies deadlines for payment and sale, as well as penalties and costs associated with tax delinquency.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the process for selling real property to collect unpaid taxes within the town of Versailles. It covers advertising, sale procedures, reporting, and the town's right to bid. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are standard for tax collection and do not appear to have any discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb V2-1868.pdf' and the year '1868'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Versailles
classification.summary: This act details procedures for the redemption of land and town lots sold for taxes in the town of Versailles. It outlines redemption periods, interest rates, and the process for handling cases where purchasers or their representatives are unavailable. It also addresses the appointment of a commissioner to convey title, the handling of insolvent or removed taxpayers, and the duties of the town marshal if there is no collector. Finally, it allows aggrieved persons to sue on the collector's bond.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the process of tax sales and redemption of property within the town of Versailles. It outlines financial and administrative procedures related to property taxes and the roles of town officials. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1868.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY, 1868
classification.summary: This section of the 1868 Laws of Kentucky details provisions related to the financial accountability of town officials, the collection of taxes, and the management of streets and alleys within the town of Versailles. It outlines procedures for settling accounts, penalties for failure to do so, and the powers of the board of trustees regarding street development and property acquisition for public use. It also specifies that the locality of streets shall not be changed without consent of the claimant of the affected ground, unless by a two-thirds vote of the board for public necessity, with just compensation provided for private property taken.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to municipal governance, specifically financial accountability of town officials and the management of public infrastructure like streets and alleys in the town of Versailles. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Cynthiana
classification.summary: This act grants the board of a town (specifically Cynthiana, as indicated by the context of the source document) the power to open, widen, or extend streets and alleys. It outlines the process for acquiring private property for these improvements, including compensation to landowners and the possibility of a writ of ad quod damnum to determine necessity and damages. The board also gains the power to keep streets free of obstructions and to pave, grade, and gutter streets at the expense of adjacent lot owners, with provisions for lien and assessment limits. Appeals are allowed to higher courts.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the powers of a town board regarding street and alley improvements, including acquisition of land, compensation, paving, and assessments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely related to municipal infrastructure and property law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Versailles
classification.summary: This statute outlines the qualifications for voting in the town of Versailles, Kentucky. It specifies that only free white male citizens, aged twenty-one or older, who have resided in the state for two years and the town for one year, and have paid their poll tax for the preceding year, are eligible to vote. It also details the process for examining poll books, determining election results, and filling vacancies in local offices.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states 'Every free white male citizen' as a requirement for voting. This is a clear indicator of racial discrimination in voting rights, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial qualifier. The category is 'voting' because the statute directly addresses voter qualifications. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1868'.

able by or forthe use of said town ; and the ferryproperty

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Criminal Laws of Henry County
classification.summary: This act establishes procedures for the quarterly court in Henry County, Kentucky, including the selection of grand juries twice a year, the duties of the county attorney, jury selection and compensation, and the appropriation of fines. It also clarifies that examining courts held by justices of the peace are not prohibited but that jurisdiction for bail bonds is given to the quarterly court. The act specifies that penalties for offenses within the quarterly court's jurisdiction will be the same as those in circuit courts, with an exception for petit larceny where the fine can range from one to one hundred dollars.

classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the St. Joseph's Orphan Society of Louisville. It grants the society the same power and control over children under their protection as fathers have over their children or guardians have over their wards, until the children reach the age of twenty-one. The society can receive, raise, care for, educate, and bind out children as apprentices. However, the county court of Jefferson county and the Louisville chancery court retain the power to investigate the treatment of children and review, correct, annul, or continue any apprenticeship articles if deemed advisable and just.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Maysville and Mt. Carmel Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act allows Lewis County to subscribe stock in the Maysville and Mt. Carmel Turnpike Road Company, similar to Mason County. It outlines provisions for appointing a treasurer and clerk, their duties and compensation, and the requirement for them to provide bond and take an oath. The company is empowered to appoint a superintendent, gate-keepers, and other employees. It also grants the company the power to pass by-laws, rules, and regulations for its governance. The act details the process for letting out road construction, determining its connection point with another road, and erecting toll-gates once a certain length of road is completed and in good repair. It specifies that toll rates will conform to general state law and be charged proportionally to the distance traveled on completed sections. The company can receive releases of right-of-way and ground for quarries and toll-houses by consent or purchase, and can initiate eminent domain proceedings if necessary.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the operational and financial aspects of a turnpike road company, including provisions for stock subscription by counties, appointment of officers, by-laws, road construction, and toll collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. 3. That the books for the subscriptionof stock shall
be opened in the town of Alexandria, and at such other
places as may be deemed best,under the directionof the
persons heretofore named ,allof whom are hereby made
commissioners,for the purpose of obtaining subscription
to the capital stock of said company ; and they shallpro-
vide a book which shallcontain the following obligation,
to be signed by every person who shall take stock in
said corporation,viz: We , whose names are hereunto sub-
scribed,do severallybind ourselvesto pay to the president
and directorsof the Alexandria and Crane Trace turnpike
road company the sum of twenty dollars for each and
every share of stock set opposite our names ,in such man-
ner and proportions,and at such times,as shallbe required
by the presidentand directorsof saidcompany ; and when
the presidentand directorsshall be chosen as heretofore
provided, they shall have fullcontrol of said books , and
may cause the same to be opened ,from time to time,until
the stock shall be subscribed .
§4 . That as soon as a majority of said commissioners
shall be of opinion that a sufficientamount of stock is
subscribedtojustifythecommencement of saidroad,they
shallcalla meeting of the stockholdersand hold an elec-
tion for president and fivedirectors, who shallhold their
officesfor one year ,and untiltheir successors are chosen :
Provided, That should a vacancy occur, the remaining
directorsshall fillthe same by appointment ; and should
the officeof all the directors become vacant , the stockhold-
ers may call a meeting to elect their successors. The
directors shall have power to appoint a secretary and
treasurer; to require them to give bond for the perform-
ance of their duties .
§5 That said road shallnot exceed thirtyfeet,but may
be reduced to twenty at cuts and fills- mettle not lessthan
pine feet in width ; and when two and one half miles of
said road iscompleted ,the company may erect a gate or
gates at such points as they deem proper, and charge the
usual ratesof toll, to be applied to the completion of said
road .
§6 . The provisions of the Revised Statutes regulating
turnpike companies , and not inconsistent with the provis-
ions of this act, shall be part hereof .
§ 7. This act to take effectfrom itspassage .
Al proved March 7,1808 .

classification.title: An Act to amend and establish roads
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for establishing and maintaining state roads in Kentucky. It details the roles and responsibilities of commissioners appointed to survey and locate routes, report to county courts, and designate areas for bridges. It also specifies how roads will be laid out into precincts, assigned surveyors, and maintained. The act addresses compensation for commissioners and procedures for land acquisition through writs of ad quod damnum. Notably, it includes a provision where commissioners from one county may not act if commissioners from other counties make a specific determination regarding the road's route in another county.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Incorporate the City of Louisville," Approved March 12, 1849, and the Several Acts Amendatory Thereto.
classification.summary: This act grants the Louisville City Council broad powers to regulate dogs within the city. These powers include imposing taxes on dogs, establishing fines and penalties for non-payment, and requiring registration. The council can also pass ordinances for the impounding, forfeiture, or killing of dogs found running at large contrary to ordinance, or allow for their redemption or purchase by others. Additionally, the act addresses the disposition of fines and recoveries in the city court of Louisville, dedicating them to the city after deducting costs for court officers and juries.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details regulations concerning dogs within the city of Louisville, including taxation, registration, impounding, and forfeiture. It also outlines the allocation of fines collected by the city court. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The regulations are purely animal control and municipal finance related. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the organization and government of the city of Louisville," approved March 10, 1868.
classification.summary: This statute details the financial responsibilities of the city of Louisville in relation to its courts, including the payment of salaries and fees for judges and officers, as well as the costs of court operations. It also outlines how fines and recoveries are to be managed, with a portion going to the city's university and public schools. Section 16 and 17 specifically address the commitment of individuals to the city workhouse for the satisfaction of fines or in lieu of bail, allowing for labor both inside and outside the workhouse. However, it specifies that individuals committed in default of bail to keep the peace or for trial will not be made to labor unless they elect to do so.
classification.reasoning: The statute primarily concerns the financial administration of the Louisville city court and the management of individuals committed to the city workhouse. While it discusses the use of labor for those committed to the workhouse, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The provisions for labor are applied generally to 'parties' committed to the workhouse, without any racial distinction. Therefore, it does not appear to be a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Louisville
classification.summary: This act details changes to the city charter of Louisville, specifically concerning the designation and compensation of the prosecuting attorney. It also outlines procedures for temporary appointments of prosecuting attorneys and the confinement of individuals arrested for violations of state or municipal law in police stationhouses.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text from the 1868 Laws of Kentucky pertains to administrative and procedural changes within the city of Louisville's legal framework. It renames the 'city attorney' to 'prosecuting attorney,' modifies their compensation structure, and allows for the use of police stationhouses for the temporary confinement of arrestees. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to the city charter of Louisville, focusing on the powers of the general council regarding licensing, public works (wells, cisterns, fire-plugs), and sidewalk construction and repair. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is on municipal governance and infrastructure. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville
classification.summary: This act grants the Louisville city council the power to pass ordinances for the grading and original improvement of streets and alleys. It details how the costs of these improvements are to be charged to property owners and secured by liens. It also outlines the process for passing such ordinances, including requirements for petitions from property owners and supermajority votes in the general council. Specific provisions are made for the apportionment of costs and the correction of such apportionments.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns municipal powers related to public works and property assessments. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on municipal infrastructure and finance without any racial component.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 419
classification.summary: This statute outlines procedures for municipal improvements such as grading, regrading, making, remaking, or repairing sidewalks, streets, alleys, lanes, avenues, or thoroughfares. It details how these improvements can be directed by the city engineer or as specified in ordinances. It also covers the construction of wells, cisterns, and fire-plugs. The statute specifies that the city engineer's suggestions must be approved by the council, and contracts for work, when attested by the engineer and approved by the council, serve as evidence of approval. It allows for modifications to the extent, character, specifications, and materials of the work by the city engineer, subject to equitable terms and council override. Section 29 addresses the process for receiving work by squares for improvements contracted across multiple squares, including the perfection of liens upon approved reports of completion.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses municipal infrastructure projects like street and sidewalk improvements, well digging, and cistern construction. It details the roles of the city engineer and city council in approving and overseeing these projects, including contract modifications and lien perfection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Louisville, and to reduce into one, all the acts and parts of acts relating to the government of said city.
classification.summary: This section of the statute outlines procedures for enforcing liens related to public improvements in the city of Louisville. It details how liens for work done under contract can be enforced through chancery court, including the evidence required, parties to the suit, and the court's power to correct errors in apportionment and decree sales of property to cover costs. It also addresses situations where work is not strictly in accordance with the contract but still provides value, and allows for extensions of time for contract completion.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided discusses the enforcement of liens for public improvements in the city of Louisville. It details legal procedures, contract acceptance, and the role of the court in resolving disputes related to these improvements. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the city of Louisville, and to repeal certain sections thereof.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Louisville concerning the establishment and acceptance of streets, alleys, and thoroughfares. It repeals existing provisions that required ordinances for the acceptance of new streets and establishes that the city's order to grade or improve such thoroughfares will be considered evidence of their acceptance as public and established. It also grants the general council power to require property owners who have laid out or sold property referencing new streets to submit plats for approval and recordation.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Covington
classification.summary: This act authorizes the city council of Covington to increase the revenue tax for city purposes from forty cents to sixty cents per one hundred dollars valuation of taxable property. It also empowers the city to create a sinking fund, not exceeding twenty-five cents per one hundred dollars valuation, to pay off city bonds and funded debt. Surplus general revenue, proceeds from fines, and license fees can be transferred to this sinking fund. The act also details procedures for handling delinquent tax bills.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Warsaw, in Gallatin County
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of Warsaw, Kentucky, broad powers to regulate public improvements, taxation of exhibitions, and the retail of spirituous liquors. It details procedures for property owners to make improvements, the board's power to enforce liens, and the authority to tax shows and grant or withhold liquor licenses. The board can also suppress or suspend liquor licenses for keeping disorderly houses, with notice and forfeiture provisions. The statute also outlines penalties for selling liquor under a suppressed or suspended license, classifying it as keeping a tippling-house.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Williamstown
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of Williamstown broad powers to govern the town. These powers include enacting ordinances, setting fines and punishments for offenses (with specific higher fines for disturbances of religious worship, riots, unlawful assemblies, breaches of the peace, and keeping tippling or gambling houses), declaring and abating nuisances, levying taxes, regulating meetings, filling vacancies, appointing town officials (assessor, treasurer, clerk, marshal, attorney), defining their duties and emoluments, setting their own fees, and enacting bylaws to protect property, shrubbery, tombstones, graves, and monuments in cemeteries and burying grounds within the town limits. It also mandates bonds for certain town officers.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the powers and responsibilities of the town of Williamstown's board of trustees. It covers a wide range of municipal governance functions, including law enforcement, taxation, and property management. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The penalties and regulations apply generally to all inhabitants of the town. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Falmouth, in Pendleton County
classification.summary: This act details the powers and duties of a police judge in the town of Falmouth, Pendleton County. It outlines their jurisdiction in civil and criminal matters, their power to issue judgments and executions, and their authority to punish contempts. The statute also specifies how fines and costs are to be handled, including provisions for commitment to jail and the issuance of executions against property. The police judge is also empowered to grant attachments, restraining orders, injunctions, and issue writs of habeas corpus, as well as preside over trials requiring a jury and issue writs of forcible entry and detainer.

Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

It also outlines the procedures for committing offenders to the town jail and the jurisdiction of the town's police judge and marshal.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative and penal functions of the town of Germantown, specifically regarding the establishment and operation of a local jail and the authority of its officials. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the Town of Mansville, in Taylor County
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Town of Mansville in Taylor County. It allows for the election of a police judge with jurisdiction over town by-laws and the same civil, penal, and criminal jurisdiction as justices of the peace. The town marshal's jurisdiction is also extended to be coextensive with constables in civil and penal cases. The act specifies election timing and term lengths for the police judge.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to the charter of the Town of Mansville, focusing on the establishment and powers of a police judge and the jurisdiction of the town marshal. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute is purely administrative and organizational in nature for the town government. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It clarifies that no provision of the prior act should be construed to authorize the town authorities to levy taxes on the capital stock of any incorporated bank that is already exempted from such taxation by law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the Town of Middletown
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Town of Middletown by granting its trustees the authority to annually elect various town officers (clerk, marshal, assessor, etc.), require bonds and security from them, and set their salaries. It also empowers the marshal with the authority of a conservator of the peace to enforce town by-laws and state penal laws within the town. The trustees can also remove officers for cause and fill vacancies.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative and law enforcement powers of the town of Middletown. It details the election, duties, and removal of town officers, including the marshal. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend the Charter of the Town of Munfordville."
classification.summary: This act addresses issues with the town boundaries and street usage in Munfordville, Hart County. It allows previously closed streets to remain closed, preventing the statute of limitations from running against citizens for this closure. The preamble explains that the town's large boundary, with much of it used for agriculture, led to streets being closed and the town trustees being indicted. This act aims to resolve these legal issues.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative and legal aspects of a town's boundaries and street management. It addresses issues related to land use, legal indictments against town trustees, and the application of the statute of limitations. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to prohibit the sale of intoxicating liquors in the town of Foster, Bracken County.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the sale of intoxicating liquors by retail in the town of Foster, Bracken County, and within two miles of its limits, starting March 1, 1868. Druggists and storekeepers can be licensed to sell for medicinal purposes only, requiring a written order from a physician. Selling less than five gallons at a time constitutes retail. Violators face fines, with a portion going to the informer. Physicians providing fraudulent orders are also liable. The police judge of Foster has concurrent jurisdiction with quarterly courts for offenses under this act.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the prohibition of alcohol sales in a specific town and its surrounding area. It outlines regulations for obtaining licenses, specifies conditions for medicinal sales, defines 'retailer,' and details penalties for violations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act details the powers and responsibilities of the board of trustees and the town marshal for the town of Henderson. CHAPTER 1037 .
AN ACT to improve the Navigation of Licking River.
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That William Mynhier , George Hamilton , Moses
Kirk ,Thompson S. Parks,and M. D. Martin ,any three of
whom may act,are appointed commissioners to go upon
Licking river, from its mouth to the mouth of Mason's
branch ,near Salyersville, in Magoffin county , and ascer-
tain, as near as may be , the cost of removing the mill-
dams and other obstructionswhich materially obstructthe
freenavigation of said river; and that they shallreport to
the adjourned sessionof thisGeneral Assembly next Jan-
uary : Provided, Said commissioners shall not be allowed
more than two hundred and fifty($250) dollars,and shall
not incur any additionalexpense .
§2 . This act to take effectfrom and afteritspassage .
Approved March 9, 1868.
CHAPTER 1038 .
AN ACT declaringthe North Fork of Licking River a Navigable Stream .
Be it enactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the North Fork of Licking river,which is the
line between Morgan and Rowan counties, be , and the
same is hereby , declared a navigable stream from its
mouth to Cassidie's Mills, on said stream .
§ 2. This act to take effectfrom and afteritspassage .
Approved March 9, 1868.

Be it enactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
§ 1. That a company shallbe,and the same ishereby,
establishedand incorporated,with a capitaloftwenty-five
thousand dollars,to be divided into five hundred shares of
fiftydollarseach,forthe purpose of constructinga mac-
adamized road,to begin at some pointon the Louisville
and Memphis railroad,in the town of Allensville, and run-
ning from thence along or keeping the general course of
the present county road ,on the most directand practicable
route to Elkton ,or intersectthe Elkton and Russellville
road at some suitable and convenient point on said road
near Elkton ,under the name and styleof the Allensville
and Elkton turnpike road company ; and as such shallbe a
body-politicand corporate; and by that name and style
shall be competent to contract and be contracted with,
sue and be sued,plead and be impleaded , answer and be

in this Commonwealth ; and with power to acquire,hold,
possess, use, and occupy all such real and personal estate
as may be necessary and convenient for the siteor route
of said road,for piersand abutments for allsuch bridges,
culverts,and drains,as may be necessary or required on
said road, and lotsfor tollhouses and residences(not ex-
ceeding two acres of land in each lot) for gate-keepers ;
also the necessary stone, gravel, and earth,for the con-
struction and repairs of said road ; and any and every
other species of material necessary for the construction
and repair of said road ; and to have and use a common
seal, and the same to alter and renew at pleasure; to
make and ordain allsuch by -laws as may be necessary for
the construction,promotion , and repair of said road,and
the management of itsprudential and financialconcerns,
not contrary to the Constitutionand laws of thisCommon-
wealth or of the United States .§ 2. That said company shall be , and they are hereby ,
authorized to increase the capital stock of said corpora-
tion for the purpose, and to an amount necessary in
continuing and constructing said road eastwardly from
Allensville,and branching at some convenient or suitable
point to or in the directionto Mason's Mills,and to or in
the directionof Bailey'sMills,on Red river.

graded and levelednot less than thirtyfeetnor more than
fortyfeetwide , and the macadamized partshallnot be less
than fifteenfeet wide ; and if the same be continued east-
wardly from Allensville,the grading and macadamizing
shallcontinuethe same width to the pointof branching,
1868 .

classification.title: AN ACT incorporatethe AllonemillonElktonTernpikeRoad Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Allensville and Elkton Turnpike Road Company, establishing a capital of twenty-five thousand dollars to construct a macadamized road. The road is to begin at a point on the Louisville and Memphis railroad in Allensville and run to Elkton, or intersect the Elkton and Russellville road. The company is authorized to acquire necessary land and materials, and to make by-laws for its operation. The act also allows for an increase in capital stock to extend the road eastwardly towards Mason's Mills and Bailey's Mills.
classification.reasoning: The statute clearly outlines the incorporation of a private company for the purpose of building a turnpike road. It details the company's capital, the route of the road, and its powers. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The statute also details voting rights for stockholders and the procedures for annual elections and filling vacancies.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the incorporation of a turnpike road company, outlining the process for stock subscription, election of officers, and road construction standards. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the Louisville and Bardstown Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act details the process for erecting toll gates on the Louisville and Bardstown Turnpike Road, including requirements for completion of road sections and the collection of tolls. It also allows for free passage for individuals attending church on the Sabbath, funerals, burials, elections, and mills on single horses. Furthermore, it permits counties, towns, or justices' districts to subscribe stock in the company, subject to voter approval.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the construction and operation of a turnpike road, including the erection of toll gates and the collection of tolls. It also outlines procedures for local government entities to invest in the road company. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1868.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Hodgenville and Muldrough's Hill Turnpike Road Company.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Hodgenville and Muldrough's Hill Turnpike Road Company to construct a macadamized or gravel road. It outlines a process for local districts to vote on subscribing stock to the company, with the county judge ordering an election. If a majority votes in favor, the judge enters the result, and the company can then levy taxes (a property tax and a head tax) for up to three years to pay for the subscription. Taxpayers are entitled to stock in proportion to their road taxes paid. The sheriff is responsible for collecting these taxes.
classification.reasoning: The statute incorporates a turnpike road company and details the process for local districts to vote on subscribing stock and the subsequent taxation to fund it. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the General Assembly in 1868.

classification.title: AN ACT to enable the Washington County Court to take Stock in the Turnpike Roads in said County
classification.summary: This act grants the Washington County Court the power to hold a vote among qualified voters to decide whether the court should invest in turnpike roads within the county. If a majority votes in favor, the court, along with a majority of justices of the peace, can levy an ad valorem tax not exceeding twenty cents per hundred dollars on taxable property to fund these turnpike roads. The sheriff is responsible for collecting this tax.

classification.summary: This act modifies a previous law (March 11th, 1867) concerning revenue collection for Hart County. It extends the time for Sheriff B. F. Jameson to collect and pay half of the revenue due for 1867 until June 1, 1868. It also waives the requirement for all sureties to consent of record to this extension if one surety is a non-resident, as long as the other sureties agree. Additionally, it applies the act to the revenue due for 1866 instead of 1865.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for taking Proof, by Deposition, in the Jefferson Court of Common Pleas, and authorizing the appointment of a Commissioner or Commissioners for such purpose.
classification.summary: This act outlines the procedures for taking depositions in the Jefferson Court of Common Pleas. It specifies that depositions can be taken with the written consent of parties or their attorneys, upon interrogatories or notice, and with regard to the witness's place of residence. It also grants parties the right to retake depositions or cross-examine witnesses, and allows the court to prescribe rules for taking depositions. The act authorizes the appointment and removal of commissioners to take depositions and details their compensation, including provisions for unusual length depositions or when a commissioner must travel to a sick witness. Finally, it allows the court to require witnesses to be produced at trial if justice requires, with consequences for failure to comply.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the procedural rules for taking depositions in a specific court. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment for any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

1868 . CHAPTER 1135 .
AN ACT to amend an act, entitled"An act to incorporatethe Cincin-
nati, Covington, and Cumberland Gap Railroad Company ."
Be it enactedby the General Assemblyof the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
§ 1. That the time in which the commissioners are re-
quired,by the third sectionof the act to which this is an
amendment ,to open books forthe subscriptionof stock in
saidcompany ,isherebyextendedto two and a halfyears
afterthe passage of thisamendment .
§2 . That the county courts of the counties through
which the roads named in this,and the act to which thisis
an amendment ,run,shallhave fullpower and authorityto
donate to said road company , as a bonus ,to induce capi-
taliststo make the same ,alllands lying within their re-
spectivecounties, and which belong to the same ,and also
to donate ,for said purpose ,any lands which may hereafter
accrueto,and become the propertyof,saidcounties,by
virtueof any law of thiscounty or otherwise.
§3 . That the corporatorsof saidcompany named in the
firstsectionof the act to which this isan amendment ,or
such of them as shall,at theirown expense and trouble,
procure bonuses such as are named in the thirty-fifthsec-
tion of the act to which thisis an amendment ,may retain,
as compensation for their services,such part of the bonus
obtained by them respectivelyas may not be required to
induce capitaliststo take stock thereinsufficientto build
the same ,not exceeding ten per cent.of the money bonuses
and ten per cent.of the land bonuses so obtained.
§4 . That the county courtsnamed in the second section
of thisamendment shall not have power to make the do-
nationsthereinnamed ,unlessa majorityof the qualified
voters in the county making the same shall authorize
these courts to make said donations ,and that fact is enter-
ed of record ; and such donations shall not take effect until
the said railroad is completed to the county seat of, or
throughthat part of,saidcounty over which itpasses,in
such time as shallbe fixedby the court.
§5 . That said company shall have power to branch
their road from such point as shall be by them deemed
most convenient, to make a road by the shortestroute
from Cincinnati to Knoxville or Chattanooga ,and to ex-
tend the same to the State lineat such point as shall be
agreed upon ,in order to meet any railroadthat may be
chartered by the State of Tennessee , to run from said
point to eitherKnoxville or Chattanooga .
§6 . That said company may begin their road at any
point on the Kentucky CentralRailroad, not exceeding
one mile north of Falmouth , and not exceeding one mile
south of Paris .

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Cincinnati, Covington, and Cumberland Gap Railroad Company."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act to incorporate the Cincinnati, Covington, and Cumberland Gap Railroad Company. It extends the time for commissioners to open books for stock subscriptions, grants county courts the power to donate lands as bonuses to the railroad company (subject to voter approval and completion of the railroad), allows corporators to retain a portion of obtained bonuses as compensation, and specifies the company's powers to branch its road and connect with railroads in Tennessee.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to an act incorporating a railroad company. It deals with extending deadlines for stock subscriptions, authorizing county courts to donate land as bonuses to the railroad company (with voter approval), and defining the railroad's routes and connections. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The statute also details the process for summoning a jury of inquest to determine compensation for land and materials needed for the railroad's construction and repair.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for the Louisville and Nashville Railroad Company to acquire land for its operations. It details how damages to property owners are assessed, including offsetting advantages derived from the road's construction. It also specifies procedures for land condemnation, valuation, and payment, and sets limits on the width of land that can be condemned for roadways unless specific engineering affidavits are provided.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Louisville and Portland Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Louisville and Portland Railroad Company, outlining its powers to subscribe stock, elect directors, extend railways within five miles of Louisville, acquire land and rights of way, hold and convey real estate, borrow money, and issue bonds. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

whereas, itisrepresentedto the General Assembly that
said Boner was only seventeen years old at the time; that

aged parents are worthy citizensof Pendleton county ; and
that, since servinghis time in the penitentiary, he has
always residedwith his parents in Pendleton county ,and
has liveda lifeof rectitude,and has gained the confidence
of all his neighbors, who are anxious that he should be
relievedof alldisabilitiesconsequentupon his said con-
viction ; therefore ,
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyoftheCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
That the said David R. Boner be ,and hereby is,restored
to allthe rightsof citizenship,and relievedof allhis disa-
bilitiesconsequent upon his said conviction.
§ 2. This act shalltake effectfrom itspassage .

AN ACT to incorporatetheAurora Fire InsuranceCompany .
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyoftheCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Henry A. Faber ,Jacob Krehbiel,Henry Leib-
hart,George A. A. Voigo , Henry Schroer, Charles Ulrich,
and Timothy Heineman , and theirassociatesand success-
ors, are hereby created a body -corporate and politic,by
the name and styleof "Aurora FireInsuranceCompany ;"
and by that name shallbe capable to sue and be sued,
plead and be impleaded , answer and be answered ,defend
and be defended,in allcourtsof law and equity; and with
fullpower to acquire and hold both real and personal
property; to selland convey thesame as may bestsuitthe
interestsof said company ; and to make and use a com-
mon seal,and the same to alteror renew atpleasure,and
generally to do and perform all things relative to the
objectof thisinstitution.
§2 . The capitalstockof this company shallbe fifty
thousand dollars,which may be increased to two hundred
thousand dollars,at the option of the directors, and shall
be composed of sharesof twentydollarseach,and consist
of cash,United States and State bonds ,and mortgages on
unincumbered real estate, worth double the amount of the
mortgage .
§ 3. So soon as one thousand shares of said stock issub-
scribedand paid in or secured by mortgage ,the company
shall be competent to transact all kinds of businessfor
which it is established .
§ 4. The transferof stock may be made by any stock-
holder or his legal representative,subject to such restric-
tions as the board of directors shall,from time to time ,
establish .
§ 5. The business of said company shallbe managed by
a board of directors,consistingof not lessthan seven nor
more than fifteenstockholders, to be elected as follows:
The stockholdersshallhold a meeting forthe firstelection
within one month after one thousand shares shall have been
subscribed, and on the firstor thirdMonday in January in
each year thereafter,and electby balloteleven directorsat
the firstelection, at which electiontwo or more of the in-
corporatorsshallact as judges of the election, keep a
poli-book,and declarethe personselecteddirectors; each
share of stock shall entitle the holder to one vote . The
stockholdersresidingin another cityfrom that where the
principalofficeshallbe located,may send intheirvoteby
mail ; and the directors so chosen shall serve until the
thirdMonday in January following,and untiltheirsuc-
cessors are elected and qualified. At theirfirstmeeting
after each election,said directorsshallchoose by ballota
